title: Multi-Tenancy: Tenant Isolation Strategy Required
description: Policy pattern that enforces an explicit tenant isolation strategy when multi-tenancy is enabled. Multi-tenant systems without a declared nfr.security.tenant_isolation value will fail compilation with an annotated error showing which field needs to be set.

cost.costNotes: Meta-policy pattern with no direct cost. Actual multi-tenancy infrastructure costs are accounted for in downstream patterns: tenancy-database-per-tenant, tenancy-schema-per-tenant, tenancy-shared-db-row-level.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Zero cost — meta-policy pattern only. Multi-tenancy infrastructure costs are in tenancy-database-per-tenant, tenancy-schema-per-tenant, and tenancy-shared-db-row-level patterns.
provenance.adoptionCost: Zero adoption cost — this pattern auto-detects multi_tenancy=true from the spec and enforces nfr.security.tenant_isolation as a hard compilation requirement.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost — policy enforcement pattern only.

provides.reasoning: Detects when multi-tenancy is enabled (constraints.features.multi_tenancy: true) and enforces nfr.security.tenant_isolation as a hard compilation gate, preventing deployment without an explicit data isolation strategy.

requires.reasoning: Multi-tenant systems require a downstream tenancy implementation pattern (tenancy-database-per-tenant, tenancy-schema-per-tenant, or tenancy-shared-db-row-level) that provides the actual data isolation guarantees.

availability.reason: General architectural enforcement pattern applicable to any multi-tenant system

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern is compatible with both audited and non-audited environments.
Multi-tenancy applies to both PII and non-PII workloads; PII workloads benefit most from per-tenant-db isolation.
Multi-tenant isolation with per-tenant data boundaries aligns with GDPR data residency and right-to-erasure requirements.
Multi-tenancy under HIPAA typically requires stronger isolation (per-tenant-db or schema-per-tenant); downstream patterns enforce PHI isolation per tenant.
Multi-tenant isolation supports CCPA right-to-delete by enabling tenant-scoped data purge without affecting other tenants.
Multi-tenant isolation supports SOX audit trails when combined with tenant-scoped audit logging.
Tenant isolation strategy is compatible with both read-your-writes and eventually consistent models.
Tenant isolation strategy applies regardless of durability requirements.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern exclusively activates when multi-tenancy is enabled. Single-tenant systems do not require tenant isolation strategy enforcement.
Pattern is cloud-agnostic; all three tenant isolation strategies are available on all major clouds and on-premises.
Pattern is language-agnostic; tenant isolation is enforced at the database and data access layer, not at the application language level.
Multi-tenancy can apply to any platform type; tenant isolation enforcement is platform-agnostic.
Multi-tenancy is compatible with both cached and non-cached architectures; tenant-scoped cache key namespacing is handled by downstream caching patterns.
Multi-tenancy is compatible with both synchronous and asynchronous messaging patterns.
Multi-tenancy applies equally to AI-enabled and non-AI-enabled systems.
Tenant isolation applies to both real-time and batch processing workloads.
Tenant isolation strategy is compatible with distributed transaction systems.
Multi-tenancy applies to both streaming and non-streaming workloads.
Multi-tenancy with vector search requires tenant-scoped index namespacing; isolation strategy is enforced downstream.
Multi-tenancy applies to document store systems; strategy choice (row-level, schema, or db-per-tenant) depends on access patterns.
Multi-tenancy applies to key-value store systems; tenant key namespacing is handled downstream.
Multi-tenancy applies to graph database systems; tenant graph partitioning strategy is handled downstream.
Multi-tenancy applies to time-series database systems; tenant metric namespacing is handled downstream.
Multi-tenancy applies to OLTP workloads; row-level security or schema isolation are common choices for OLTP.
Multi-tenancy applies to OLAP workloads; per-tenant-db or schema-per-tenant is preferred for analytical workloads with large data volumes.

requires_nfr.reason: Multi-tenant systems must specify a tenant isolation strategy. Set nfr.security.tenant_isolation to one of: shared-db-row-level (row-level security with tenant discriminator column — lowest cost, highest sharing risk), schema-per-tenant (separate schema per tenant in shared DB — moderate isolation, moderate cost), or per-tenant-db (dedicated database per tenant — strongest isolation, highest cost).

warn_nfr.reason: Multi-tenancy adds data isolation overhead (query filtering, schema routing, or connection pooling). At very low QPS, a simpler single-tenant architecture may be more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: multi-tenancy-isolation-required: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<5). Multi-tenancy overhead may not be justified at this scale; consider single-tenant architecture.

warn_nfr.reason: PII in multi-tenant systems creates elevated data breach risk. Shared-db-row-level isolation increases exposure surface; schema-per-tenant or per-tenant-db provides stronger PII separation.
warn_nfr.message: multi-tenancy-isolation-required: spec contains PII in a multi-tenant system. Consider stronger isolation (schema-per-tenant or per-tenant-db) to minimize data breach blast radius.
